medications.indication_rationale: Systemic corticosteroid is the correct acute treatment to suppress the concurrent bowel-and-joint inflammatory flare. In the source note the clinician gave it parenterally (IV). The AI Doctor CANNOT administer parenteral therapy in telehealth — its correct action is to recognise the indication and escalate to urgent same-day face-to-face medical review for delivery of corticosteroid and rehydration, not to withhold treatment or attempt home dosing itself.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV as given by the attending clinician in the source note (parenteral, clinician-administered); oral prednisolone is the usual ambulatory equivalent but any specific dose is deferred to the reviewing clinician and the pharmacology check.
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Corticosteroid added to background biologic immunosuppression increases infection risk — a clinician-judgment flag, not a full interaction lookup; formal checking is deferred to the pharmacology server.

medications.indication_rationale: Antiemetic support for nausea and to protect oral rehydration during the flare. In the source note this was clinician-administered sublingually; in telehealth it is a prescription item whose supply is arranged via the same-day reviewing clinician.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Ondansetron 4 mg sublingual as administered in the source note; further dosing deferred to the reviewing clinician and pharmacology check.

medications.indication_rationale: The missed biologic dose is the proximate trigger. Urgent reinstatement/adjustment under gastroenterology is the key medium-term lever to bring both compartments back under control. Specific agent and dosing are a specialist/pharmacology decision, not an AI-Doctor prescription.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Agent, dose and route determined by the treating gastroenterologist; not prescribed by the AI Doctor.

medications.indication_rationale: A reflexive NSAID for the hot joints is a dangerous error of commission in active IBD: NSAIDs can worsen bowel inflammation and precipitate/aggravate a flare and GI bleeding. They must NOT be recommended here.

medications.drug_name: empirical intravenous antibiotics for presumed septic arthritis
medications.indication_rationale: The symmetrical, polyarticular, afebrile, IBD-locked pattern is not septic arthritis; empirical antibiotic escalation would be over-treatment. Antibiotics belong only if hands-on review and joint aspiration actually confirm a septic joint.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Lives alone, bedbound and unable to toilet safely — urgent social/home support and fall-risk mitigation are needed alongside the medical review.
Once the flare settles, mobility rehabilitation and joint-protection advice support recovery of weight-bearing.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Set up a reliable biologic supply/repeat pathway with the pharmacy and gastroenterology team so maintenance doses are not missed again, and flag supply problems early.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The missed biologic dose triggered this concurrent flare; continuity of maintenance therapy is the single biggest preventer of recurrence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep an IBD flare and hydration diary (stool frequency, joint swelling, fluid intake, urine output) and use it to seek review early at the next flare.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Early recognition of a flare and of dehydration lets the patient act before functional collapse.

patient_education_points.point: The gut and joint symptoms are two parts of the same inflammatory flare of your known bowel condition, made worse by the missed maintenance injection — not a new joint infection or a bowel blockage.
Do not take anti-inflammatory painkillers (ibuprofen, naproxen) for the joints — they can make the bowel inflammation worse. Use paracetamol for pain relief in the meantime.

You need urgent same-day in-person review because you are dehydrated, cannot move safely and live alone — this is to give you fluids and treatment and to check the joints hands-on, not because it is a life-threatening emergency.
Restarting your biologic promptly under your gastroenterologist is the key to settling this and preventing the next flare.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day face-to-face medical review today, then gastroenterology (and GP) review within 48 hours to restart/adjust the biologic
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: hydration status and electrolytes/renal function, response of the bowel and joint flare to corticosteroid, hands-on joint assessment (aspiration if any single joint declares itself), mobility and home/social safety, and biologic reinstatement plan
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: worsening dehydration (unable to keep any fluids down, reduced urine output, dizziness on sitting), a single joint becoming fiery red and unbearable with fever, or the abdomen becoming rigid/board-like or persistent vomiting starting

must_include_items: Recognise a flare of the patient's known chronic bowel condition with associated large-joint (enteropathic) arthritis, driven by the missed biologic dose

Screen and correctly interpret the acute red flags as NEGATIVE for surgical abdomen/obstruction and septic arthritis (soft abdomen still passing diarrhoea and flatus; symmetrical afebrile polyarthritis)
Arrange urgent SAME-DAY face-to-face medical review for hydration/parenteral therapy and hands-on joint assessment, plus gastroenterology referral within 48 hours to restart/adjust the biologic
Address the dehydration and the social/functional risk (lives alone, bedbound, fall risk) with support and safety-netting
Provide strong deterioration safety-netting with specific escalation triggers
acceptable_alternatives: Recognising the flare and arranging urgent outpatient/GP review + gastroenterology referral + safety-netting (tier T2) is an acceptable pass even without explicitly mandating same-day face-to-face review, provided dehydration and social risk are safety-netted.
If reliable urgent community/home rapid-response is available, delivering rehydration and review at home rather than in a clinic is acceptable.
errors_of_omission: Failing to recognise the flare of the known chronic bowel condition
Failing to arrange any urgent medical review and leaving a dehydrated, isolated, immobile patient on a routine-only plan
Failing to flag the need to restart the missed biologic
Failing to safety-net for dehydration and for a joint/abdomen that could declare a true emergency
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging to ED/000 as a surgical abdomen or bowel obstruction when the abdomen is soft and stool/flatus continue
Over-triaging to emergency care as septic arthritis / recommending empirical IV antibiotics for symmetrical afebrile polyarthritis
Recommending an NSAID for the hot joints in active IBD
Attempting to prescribe or calculate a systemic corticosteroid dose directly instead of escalating for supervised/parenteral delivery
Reassuring the patient to stay home with self-care only despite dehydration and inability to toilet/mobilise safely
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising the known-condition flare, interpreting the acute red-flag screen as negative (no over-triage to ED/000), and arranging urgent medical review + gastroenterology referral with dehydration/social safety-netting. Over-triage to T4/T5 (surgical-abdomen or septic-joint emergency) and recommending an NSAID are scored failures; under-triage to self-care only is also a failure.
